notes.note: Cicero's scriba (a salaried public clerk, here his personal accounts-secretary), who drew up the official account-books of the Cilician proconsulship. He happens to share Cicero's nomen Tullius but is no relation; Mescinius suspected him of mishandling the figures.

Rationes referre: the legally required submission of a provincial governor's financial accounts after his term. The whole letter is a point-by-point defence of how Cicero closed and filed the Cilician accounts.

notes.anchor: under the Julian law the accounts had to be left in the province and to be entered word for word at the treasury
notes.note: Caesar's extortion law of 59 BC (lex Iulia de repetundis) required a governor to deposit copies of his accounts in two cities of his province and to lodge an identical copy at the Treasury (aerarium) in Rome. This double filing is the procedural backbone of Cicero's whole defence.

M. Mindius, brother of L. Mescinius, whom Mescinius attached to Cicero's secretary to oversee the closing of the accounts. The book was handed back to Cicero from Mindius's hands, Cicero stresses, exactly as from the secretary's.

The two leading assize-centres (conventus) of Cicero's province where, as the lex Iulia required, he deposited the completed and collated account-books. Laodicea ad Lycum and Apamea Cibotus were the chief Phrygian cities administratively attached to Cilicia.

Praedes Valeriani: the personal guarantors who stood security for the contractor Valerius, under whose name the money had been advanced. Cicero's experts ruled the debt could not be shifted to Volusius, so these sureties stayed legally liable.

notes.anchor: the sum 3{,}000{,}000 sesterces, as you write, but 19{,}000
notes.note: A correction of Mescinius's figure: the sum at issue was not HS 3,000,000 but only HS 19,000 (the surviving balance Cicero booked). Sums in the letters are reckoned in sesterces (HS), the small silver-then-bronze coin that was the standard unit of Roman account.

notes.anchor: my legate and prefect Q. Lepta
notes.note: Q. Paconius Lepta served Cicero in Cilicia as praefectus fabrum (chief of engineers/staff officer); Cicero here also styles him a legate. His freeing from a heavy financial liability was, Cicero insists, his own doing and not the secretary's favour.

notes.anchor: the money in question was deposited in a temple
notes.note: Public funds were routinely lodged for safekeeping in a temple, whose sanctity gave a quasi-legal deposit; here the deposit was made on Pompey's authority and Cicero's order. Pompey later drew on it, an awkward fact in 49 BC as civil war loomed.

notes.anchor: they had to be named within the thirty days in which the accounts were rendered
notes.note: Beneficia (the formal commendation of officers for state rewards) had to be registered within the thirty-day window allowed for filing the accounts. Cicero pleads he misjudged the deadline and so lost the chance to name some of Mescinius's nominees.

notes.anchor: deposited at Ephesus with the publicans
notes.note: Cicero placed the lawful surplus of his province on deposit with the companies of tax-farmers (publicani) at Ephesus, who functioned as bankers across the eastern provinces. Pompey then requisitioned the whole sum for the war effort.

HS 2,200,000, the entire lawfully accumulated surplus of Cicero's Cilician command, carried off by Pompey. Cicero uses the larger loss to argue that Mescinius should bear the comparatively trivial HS 100,000 dispute with equanimity.
